column11: The Asian Brown Flycatcher is a plain, medium-sized bird characterized by its overall brownish plumage, a prominent white eyering, and a large-eyed appearance. It has an unmarked throat and underparts, but juvenile birds display buff-spotted upperparts and a breast with faint scaling. The species has shorter, plainer wings compared to similar brown flycatchers and is known for flicking its tail and wings when perched. It prefers broadleaf forests and is often found in more open areas during migration.
column12: Males may have a slightly more vivid or striking plumage, although this is not a consistent or significant difference.
column13: Females may show a slightly paler or more grayish throat and a generally duller, more uniform underparts compared to males.
column15: The Asian Brown Flycatcher is a breeding summer visitor to Sikkim, Arunachal Pradesh, and the northeast Indian states like Assam, Nagaland, Meghalaya, Manipur, and Mizoram. It winters in peninsular India, particularly the Western Ghats and the midlands of Southwest India, with some birds also found in the Andaman and Nicobar Islands. During the non-breeding season, it can also be found as a passage migrant throughout the peninsula and in parts of the Indo-Gangetic plains.

column11: The Asian koel is a large, cuckoo-family bird recognized by its distinct sexual dimorphism: males are glossy black with a pale bill, while females are brown, speckled with white, and have cream-colored underparts with dark bars. Both sexes share striking crimson eyes and a long tail, but are generally secretive, found in dense trees across South and Southeast Asia, and are known for their characteristic loud "ko-el" call. A brood parasite, the koel lays its eggs in other birds' nests, often crows', leaving them to raise the young koel chicks.
column12: Plumage: Glossy blue-black. Eyes: Striking crimson. Bill: Dull lime-green or pale. Tail: Long.

column11: Asian Openbill is a medium-sized stork, known for its distinctive greyish-white plumage with glossy black wings and tail. Its most recognizable feature is the open gap between the upper and lower mandibles of its dull, yellow-grey bill, a unique adaptation for feeding on snails.
column12: Males have dark-colored irises.
column13: Females have yellow irises.
column15: The Asian Openbill stork is widespread throughout India, especially in wetland and agricultural areas of the North Indian plains (from Gujarat to Assam), the East and South Indian Peninsula, West Bengal, and Kerala

column11: The Alexandrine Parakeet, also known as the Alexandrine Parrot, is one of the largest parakeets in Asia. It has predominantly green plumage, a long tail tipped with yellow, and a large red beak with a paler tip. A distinctive maroon patch is present on the shoulders, and males display a rose-pink collar with a black stripe across the lower cheek. Females and juveniles lack the collar and are generally duller. This species is named after Alexander the Great, who is said to have transported these birds from Punjab to Europe. It inhabits forests, woodlands, plantations, and urban parks.
column12: Males are bright green with a rose-pink collar and black cheek stripe, plus a maroon shoulder patch.
column13: Females are duller, lack the collar and cheek stripe, and have shorter tails. Juveniles resemble females.
column14: Loud, harsh screams and screeches, often heard in flight or when perched in flocks.
column15: Native to South and Southeast Asia: widespread across India, Sri Lanka, Afghanistan, Pakistan, Myanmar, Thailand, and the Andaman Islands. Common in northern India and large flocks occur in the Andamans. Declining in Sri Lanka.

column11: The Ashy Woodswallow, also called the Ashy Swallow-shrike, is a compact bird with ashy-grey plumage, a darker head, and a narrow pale rump band. Its underside is soft pinkish-grey, and the short tail is slaty black tipped with white. It has a silvery finch-like bill, long triangular wings, and short legs. Often seen perched in tight groups on wires or tall trees, especially palms. Juveniles show barring on the underside.
column12: Males and females look alike, both showing the same ashy-grey plumage and maroon shoulder patch absent.
column13: Females are indistinguishable from males; no sexual dimorphism noted.
column14: Loud, harsh chattering calls; soft twittering when in flocks.
column15: Found across South Asia including India, Sri Lanka, Nepal, Bangladesh, Myanmar, and Thailand. Common in open country, farmlands, plantations, and urban areas.
column17: No recognized subspecies; plumage uniform across range.

column11: The Asian Green Bee-eater is a slender, brightly colored bird with vivid green plumage, long pointed wings, and elongated central tail feathers. It has a narrow black throat band, a golden-green crown, and a black eye-stripe that enhances its striking appearance. The bill is long, slightly curved, adapted for catching insects mid-air. Often seen perched on wires or exposed branches, it makes swift sallies to snatch bees, wasps, and other flying insects.
column12: Males are similar to females but often show longer central tail streamers and slightly brighter plumage.
column13: Females are nearly identical to males, though tail streamers are shorter and plumage may appear a little duller.
column14: Sharp, repetitive “trill” or “tree-tree” calls, often heard in flocks.
column15: Widely distributed across South Asia, including India, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, Pakistan, Nepal, and Myanmar. Common in open countryside, farmlands, scrublands, and urban areas.

column11: The Asian Palm-Swift is a slender, fast-flying bird with long, narrow wings and a deeply forked tail. Its plumage is pale brownish-grey above and whitish below, blending well with open skies. It is closely associated with palm trees, where it builds nests attached to hanging fronds using saliva. The species is highly aerial, spending most of its life in flight, feeding on small insects caught mid-air.
column12: Males and females are alike in plumage, both showing slender bodies and forked tails.
column13: No sexual dimorphism; females resemble males.
column14: High-pitched twittering and rapid squeaky calls, often heard in flight.
column15: Found widely across South and Southeast Asia, including India, Sri Lanka, Bangladesh, Myanmar, Thailand, Cambodia, and Vietnam. Common in open country, farmlands, and urban areas with palms.
column17: C. b. balasiensis, C. b. infumatus

column11: The Asian Pied Starling, also known as the Pied Myna, is a medium-sized bird with striking black-and-white plumage and a bright orange-red bill with a yellow base. It has a glossy black head, neck, and upperparts, contrasting with white face patches and underparts. The wings are black with white patches visible in flight, and the tail is black tipped with white. It is highly social, often seen in pairs or small flocks, and is known for its melodious, varied calls.
column12: Males and females are similar in plumage, both showing the same black-and-white contrast and bright bill.
column13: Females are indistinguishable from males; no sexual dimorphism noted.
column14: Rich, varied whistles, chatters, and melodious notes; often mimics other sounds.
column15: Widely distributed across South Asia, including India, Nepal, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, and Myanmar. Common in farmlands, villages, towns, and open countryside.

column11: The Cinereous Tit, also known as the Asian Tit, is a small, active bird with a black head, throat, and bib, contrasting with white cheeks and underparts. Its upperparts are greyish, and the wings show pale edging. It is agile, often seen hopping among branches or hanging upside down while foraging for insects, seeds, and small fruits. It is a common garden and woodland bird, frequently visiting feeders and nesting in tree cavities or artificial nest boxes.
column12: Males are similar to females but may show slightly glossier black plumage on the head and bib.
column13: Females resemble males, though the black bib may be narrower and less glossy.
column14: Loud, varied calls including “chee-chee” notes and scolding chatter; also produces a rapid trill.
column15: Widely distributed across South Asia, including India, Nepal, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, and Myanmar. Found in gardens, forests, plantations, and urban areas.

column11: The Baya Weaver is a small, sparrow-sized bird famous for its intricately woven hanging nests. It has a stout conical bill and a preference for colonies near water bodies, grasslands, and agricultural fields. During breeding, males develop bright yellow plumage on the crown and breast, contrasting with brown upperparts and streaked underparts. Outside the breeding season, both sexes appear dull brown and resemble sparrows. Their nests, suspended from palm fronds or thorny trees, are masterpieces of weaving, with a long vertical entrance tube.
column12: Breeding males show bright yellow crown, breast, and darker mask with strong contrast against brown wings and streaked underparts.
column13: Females and non-breeding males are dull brown, streaked, and sparrow-like, lacking the bright yellow crown.
column14: A series of harsh “chee-chee” notes and chattering calls, often heard around colonies.
column15: Widely distributed across South and Southeast Asia, including India, Nepal, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, Myanmar, Thailand, and Cambodia. Common in farmlands, grasslands, wetlands, and villages.

column11: The Bengal Bushlark is a small, inconspicuous bird with streaked brown upperparts, pale underparts with fine streaking, and a short tail. It has a heavy bill and rounded wings, adapted for short flights. Often seen on the ground or low shrubs, it blends well with dry grasslands and scrub habitats. During breeding, males perform aerial song flights, rising and fluttering while delivering melodious notes.
column12: Males are similar in plumage but perform distinctive display flights and may show slightly richer tones.
column13: Females resemble males, with no marked differences in plumage.
column14: Rich, varied warbling song delivered during aerial displays; also produces short chirps and whistles.
column15: Found across the Indian subcontinent, including India, Bangladesh, Nepal, and Sri Lanka. Common in dry grasslands, scrublands, farmlands, and open countryside.

column11: The Black Bittern is a medium-sized heron-like bird with dark, sooty-black plumage and a long neck. It has a distinctive golden-yellow throat and sides of the neck, contrasting with its otherwise dusky appearance. The bill is long and pointed, adapted for spearing fish and aquatic prey. It is secretive, often concealed in dense reed beds or mangroves, and flies with rapid wingbeats when flushed.
column12: Males are similar to females, both showing dark plumage with golden throat markings.
column13: Females resemble males; no marked sexual dimorphism. Juveniles are browner with streaked plumage.
column14: Harsh croaking and booming calls, usually heard at dusk or night.
column15: Found across South and Southeast Asia, including India, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, Nepal, Myanmar, Thailand, Cambodia, Indonesia, and the Philippines. Prefers wetlands, marshes, reed beds, and mangroves.
column17: No distinct subspecies widely recognized; plumage consistent across range.

column11: The Black Drongo is a medium-sized, entirely black bird with a distinctive forked tail and glossy plumage. It is fearless and aggressive, often mobbing larger raptors and protecting its territory. Known as the “farmer’s friend,” it feeds on insects and is commonly seen perched on wires, poles, or open branches in agricultural landscapes. Its flight is agile and graceful, with frequent sallies to catch prey mid-air.

column13: Females resemble males but may appear slightly duller; juveniles are brownish-black with less gloss.
column14: Harsh, metallic “chee-chee” notes and varied whistles; alarm calls are sharp and scolding.

column11: The Black-naped Oriole is a strikingly beautiful bird with bright golden-yellow plumage, contrasting black wings edged with yellow, and a distinctive black stripe running from the bill through the eye to the nape. It has a pinkish bill and red eyes, making it highly conspicuous. Agile and arboreal, it is often seen in the canopy of tall trees, feeding on fruits, berries, insects, and nectar. Its melodious fluting calls are a hallmark of summer woodlands.
column12: Males are vivid golden-yellow with a bold black eye-stripe and nape, plus brighter plumage overall.
column13: Females are slightly duller, with more greenish tones on the back and less vivid yellow underparts. Juveniles are streaked and less brightly colored.
column14: Rich, fluty whistles and melodious notes, often repeated in series.
column15: Widely distributed across South and Southeast Asia, including India (northeast and eastern states), Bangladesh, Myanmar, Thailand, Cambodia, Vietnam, and the Philippines. In India, it is a summer visitor to the northeast and migrates locally.

column11: The Black-rumped Flameback, also known as the Lesser Golden-backed Woodpecker, is a striking bird with golden-yellow upperparts, a black rump, and bold black-and-white facial markings. It has a long, straight bill adapted for drilling into wood, and a stiff tail that supports it while climbing tree trunks. The species is noisy and conspicuous, often drumming on trees and calling loudly. It inhabits open forests, groves, plantations, and urban areas.

column13: Females resemble males but have a black crest streaked with white instead of crimson. Juveniles are duller with streaked plumage.
column14: Loud, sharp “ki-ki-ki” calls and rapid drumming on wood.
column15: Widely distributed across the Indian subcontinent, including India, Nepal, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, and Pakistan. Common in open woodlands, farmlands, and urban parks.

column11: The Black-winged Kite is a small, graceful raptor with pale grey and white plumage, striking red eyes, and bold black patches on the shoulders and wings. It is known for its hovering flight, often seen suspended in the air while scanning for prey. Its diet mainly consists of rodents, small reptiles, and large insects, making it a beneficial bird in agricultural landscapes.

column13: Females resemble males; juveniles are buffy with streaked plumage and less distinct wing markings.
column14: High-pitched whistles and harsh “kree-kree” calls, especially during breeding season.

column11: The Blue-throated Barbet is a brightly colored bird with vivid green plumage, a turquoise-blue throat and face, crimson forehead and crown, and yellowish streaks on the sides of the head. It has a stout bill adapted for fruit-eating and is often heard before being seen, thanks to its loud, repetitive calls. It inhabits wooded areas, orchards, and gardens, feeding mainly on fruits, berries, and insects.

column11: The Bluethroat is a small, striking songbird known for its vivid throat patch. It has brown upperparts, a white supercilium, and reddish flanks. The male’s throat is bright blue bordered by black and rust-red, often with a central white or rufous spot depending on subspecies. It is a ground-dwelling bird, often flicking its tail to reveal rufous patches. It breeds in wetlands and scrub across Europe and Asia, migrating to South Asia in winter.

column14: Rich, varied warbling song with mimicry; sharp “chak” call when alarmed.

column11: Black-crowned Night Heron is a stocky, medium-sized wading bird with a distinctive black back and crown, gray wings, and white underparts, bright red eyes, and short greenish-yellow legs that turn pink in the breeding season. Adults have long white plumes that extend from their heads, while immatures are brown with white spots and streaks. These nocturnal birds roost in trees by day and are active at dusk, foraging in various aquatic habitats for fish, insects, and other small animals.

column11: The Black-headed Ibis is a large, predominantly white wading bird with a black head and neck, a long, down-curved black bill, and grayish tail feathers that become black in the breeding season. During breeding, bare wing patches turn blood-red, and the head may acquire a blueish tinge or a red neck patch. Juveniles are distinguishable by grayish neck feathers and brown-speckled wings.

column11: Black-hooded Oriole is a striking medium-sized bird primarily known for its vibrant yellow plumage contrasting with a distinct black hood, black flight feathers, and a black tail center. The male is predominantly yellow with a black head, wings, and tail, while the female is a duller greenish color but also possesses the black hood.
column12: The male Black-hooded Oriole is bright golden-yellow across the body, contrasted sharply by a glossy, solid black hood. Its wings and tail also bear black markings, which enhance the striking contrast between the vivid yellow and deep black, making the male’s plumage highly eye-catching.
column13: The female Black-hooded Oriole has a more subdued appearance, with yellow-green underparts and a duller, less distinct black hood compared to the male. Her overall plumage is muted and drabber, serving an important purpose: the duller colors provide effective camouflage while she is nesting.
column15: The Black-hooded Oriole is a widespread resident across most of India, especially in central, eastern, northeastern, and southern regions. It is seen year-round but shows seasonal movements, with winter records expanding into the northern plains and Himalayan foothills.

column11: The Blue-throated Flycatcher (Cyornis rubeculoides) is a small, insectivorous bird from the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia, about 15 cm long, known for its sexually dimorphic plumage. Adult males have a vibrant blue head, throat, and upper breast, an orange-buff band on the chest, and a white belly. Females are duller, with olive-brown upperparts, a paler blue or orange-tinged throat, and a white belly. They are found in dense scrub and forest habitats, are agile hunters of flying insects, and possess melodious songs and distinctive calls
column12: The male Blue-throated Flycatcher has bright blue upperparts, with the throat and breast also showing an extensive, vivid blue coloration. This striking contrast is balanced by a clean white belly, giving the male a vibrant and eye-catching appearance.
column13: The female Blue-throated Flycatcher is more subdued, with duller olive-brown upperparts and a paler, less defined orange-cream coloring on the throat and breast. Like the male, she has a white belly, but her tail is often rufous, adding a reddish-brown touch. She also shows a weak eyering, which further helps in distinguishing her from the male.
column15: The Blue-throated Flycatcher's distribution in India includes its breeding range in the Himalayan foothills (e.g., Arunachal Pradesh, Assam, and other eastern Himalayan regions) and its non-breeding, or wintering, grounds in southern and central India, particularly the Western Ghats and the entire Indian peninsula where suitable forest habitats are found.

column11: The Bronze-winged Jacana is a distinctive, medium-sized wader found in South and Southeast Asia, characterized by its incredibly long toes that allow it to walk on floating aquatic vegetation. It has striking black plumage, an iridescent bronze-green back and wings, a chestnut-colored tail, and a prominent white stripe from its eye to its nape. A unique feature is a red, coot-like frontal shield on its bill.
column12: The male is smaller and lighter than the female. He is responsible for incubating the eggs, building new nests if necessary for safety, and caring for the chicks after they hatch. While females establish the overall territory, males also defend their individual areas within the female’s larger territory, contributing to the protection and success of their offspring.
column13: he female is significantly larger and heavier than the male, weighing between 226–355 g compared to the male’s 147–202 g. She plays a dominant role in the social structure, being more aggressive and responsible for establishing and defending territories that may contain one to four males.
column15: The Bronze-winged Jacana is widespread throughout India, inhabiting the lowlands and plateau regions.

column11: The Brown Shrike is a medium-sized, long-tailed bird found mainly in Asia, recognizable by its distinctive black "bandit-mask" through the eye, a white eyebrow stripe, and overall brown plumage. It is an opportunistic predator that hunts from exposed perches in open, scrubby habitats, impaling its prey like insects, lizards, and smaller birds on thorns or sharp twigs. While the adult male has a dark brown crown and upperparts with a yellowish-buff underside, females and immature birds are often less contrasted, with greyer crowns and scalloped underparts.
column12: The male has a prominent and well-defined black facial mask that extends from the beak to the ear-coverts, giving it a sharp, contrasting look. Its crown and upperparts are usually pale grey or dark brown, while the underparts are paler, often yellowish-buff or creamy.
column13: The female has a less distinct mask, usually darker brown rather than solid black. Her crown is more brownish-grey and lacks the contrast seen in males. The underparts may show fine barring or scalloping, giving a more patterned appearance.
column15: The Brown Shrike is a common winter migrant to southern, central, and eastern India. While rare in the north and northwest

column11: The Cinnamon Bittern is a small, secretive heron-like bird with rich cinnamon-brown plumage, a short neck, and a pointed bill. It is often concealed in reed beds and marsh vegetation, where it feeds on fish, frogs, and aquatic insects. In flight, its wings appear contrastingly darker, and its flight is rapid and low over water. It is more often heard than seen, especially at dawn and dusk.

column13: Females are similar but slightly duller; juveniles are streaked brown and buff, providing camouflage in reeds.

column18: Yellow Bittern (Ixobrychus sinensis), Black Bittern (Ixobrychus flavicollis)

column11: The Chestnut-tailed Starling, also known as the Grey-headed Myna, is a medium-sized starling with pale grey head, whitish underparts, and chestnut-colored tail and rump. It has a yellow bill tipped with black and pale eyes. Highly social, it is often seen in flocks feeding on fruits, nectar, and insects. It is a canopy-dwelling bird, frequently visiting flowering and fruiting trees.
column12: Males have a pale grey head, whitish throat, and chestnut rump and tail, with slightly glossier plumage.
column13: Females are similar but may appear duller and less glossy. Juveniles are browner with streaked plumage.
column14: Soft whistles, chatters, and melodious notes; often calls in flocks.
column15: Widely distributed across South Asia, including India, Nepal, Bangladesh, Bhutan, and Myanmar. Found in forests, groves, plantations, and urban gardens.
column17: S. m. malabarica, S. m. nemoricola
column18: Asian Pied Starling (Gracupica contra), Jungle Myna (Acridotheres fuscus)

column8: Migratory (breeds in West Asia, winters in South Asia)
column11: The Clamorous Reed-Warbler is a large, robust warbler with plain brown upperparts, paler underparts, and a long, heavy bill. It is strongly associated with reed beds, marshes, and wetlands, where it skulks among vegetation. True to its name, it has a loud, harsh, and repetitive song, often delivered from exposed perches. It feeds on insects, spiders, and small aquatic invertebrates.
column12: Males are similar to females but sing loudly and persistently during breeding season.
column13: Females resemble males; juveniles are paler and more streaked.
column14: Loud, harsh, repetitive “charr-charr” and “krr-krr” notes; song is strong and unmusical.
column15: Found across South Asia, West Asia, and parts of Southeast Asia. In India and Bangladesh, it is a winter visitor to reed beds, marshes, and wetlands.
column17: A. s. brunnescens, A. s. meridionalis, A. s. stentoreus
column18: Great Reed-Warbler (Acrocephalus arundinaceus), Paddyfield Warbler (Acrocephalus agricola)

column11: The Common Iora is a small, lively bird with bright plumage and a melodious song. It is often seen flitting among foliage in gardens, forests, and plantations. Males show striking seasonal plumage, while females are more subdued. It feeds on insects, caterpillars, and small fruits, often foraging actively in pairs or small groups.
column12: Breeding males are bright yellow below with black upperparts, wings, and tail; non-breeding males are greener above with less contrast.
column13: Females are greenish above and yellowish below, lacking the bold black of breeding males. Juveniles resemble females.
column14: Sweet, whistling notes and varied melodious calls; males sing prominently during breeding season.
column15: Widely distributed across South and Southeast Asia, including India, Bangladesh, Nepal, Sri Lanka, Myanmar, Thailand, Cambodia, and Indonesia. Found in forests, gardens, and plantations.
column17: A. t. tiphia, A. t. humei, A. t. philipi, A. t. septentrionalis
column18: Marshall’s Iora (Aegithina nigrolutea), Green Iora (Aegithina viridissima)

column11: The Common Myna is a medium-sized, highly adaptable bird with brown body, glossy black head, and bright yellow bare skin around the eyes. It has yellow legs and bill, and white wing patches visible in flight. Known for its boldness and intelligence, it thrives in urban, rural, and agricultural landscapes. It feeds on insects, fruits, grains, and scraps, often foraging near human habitation.
column12: Males and females are alike, both showing brown plumage, black head, and yellow eye-patch.
column13: Females resemble males; juveniles are duller with less gloss and paler bare skin.
column14: Loud, varied calls including harsh “kree-kree,” whistles, and chatters; excellent mimic.
column15: Widely distributed across South Asia, including India, Nepal, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, Pakistan, and Myanmar. Introduced populations thrive in Australia, the Middle East, and many Pacific islands.
column17: A. t. tristis, A. t. melanosternus
column18: Jungle Myna (Acridotheres fuscus), Bank Myna (Acridotheres ginginianus)

column11: The Common Tailorbird is a small, active bird famous for its unique nest-building technique, stitching leaves together with plant fibers or spider silk. It has bright green upperparts, whitish underparts, and a long tail often held upright. The bird is restless, flitting through shrubs and gardens, feeding on insects and small invertebrates.
column12: Males have a rufous forehead and crown, green upperparts, and long tail feathers, especially during breeding season.
column13: Females are similar but with shorter tail feathers; juveniles are duller and lack the rufous crown.
column14: Loud, sharp “cheeup-cheeup” notes and repetitive calls; often heard more than seen.
column15: Widely distributed across South and Southeast Asia, including India, Bangladesh, Nepal, Sri Lanka, Myanmar, Thailand, Cambodia, and Indonesia. Common in gardens, scrublands, and urban areas.
column17: O. s. sutorius, O. s. guatemalensis, O. s. patia, O. s. longicauda
column18: Ashy Prinia (Prinia socialis), Plain Prinia (Prinia inornata)

column11: The Coppersmith Barbet is a small, stout bird with green upperparts, yellow underparts, and a crimson forehead and throat patch. It has a thick bill and red eyes. Its name comes from its monotonous “tuk-tuk” call, resembling a coppersmith striking metal. It feeds mainly on fruits, figs, berries, and occasionally insects. Commonly seen in gardens, orchards, and wooded areas.
column12: Males have crimson forehead, throat, and more vivid plumage.
column13: Females are similar but may show duller coloration; juveniles are streaked and lack bright red patches.
column14: Loud, repetitive “tuk-tuk-tuk” notes, often heard throughout the day.
column15: Widely distributed across South and Southeast Asia, including India, Bangladesh, Nepal, Sri Lanka, Myanmar, Thailand, Cambodia, and Indonesia. Common in urban gardens, orchards, and forests.
column17: P. h. haemacephalus, P. h. indicus, P. h. delicae, P. h. roseus
column18: Blue-throated Barbet (Psilopogon asiaticus), Brown-headed Barbet (Psilopogon zeylanicus)

column11: The Cattle Egret is a stocky, medium-sized white wader known for its short legs, thick neck, and hunched posture, distinguishing it from other egrets. Adults are white year-round but develop bright orange-buff plumes on their crown, chest, and back during the breeding season, at which time their bills and legs also turn bright red. They are often seen following cattle and other livestock in fields, feeding on insects stirred up by their movements.
column12: The male Cattle Egret is typically larger than the female in most body measurements and may have a slightly larger, more robust bill. During the breeding season, males display more pronounced and intense orange-buff plumes on the head, breast, and lower back, making them appear more striking. Their breeding colors are also generally deeper and more vivid than those of females.
column13: The female Cattle Egret is usually smaller and more delicate in build compared to the male. Her bill is slimmer, and during the breeding season, she shows orange-buff plumes as well, though they are less pronounced and paler than in males. Overall, her coloration is softer and less intense, giving her a subtler appearance.
column15: Cattle Egrets are found throughout India, with seasonal movements observed, particularly from the cooler North to the warmer South and vice versa, in response to monsoons and breeding seasons.
column17: Monotype
column18:
Little Egret 
Great Egret
Intermediate Egret

column11: The Citrine Wagtail is a small songbird with a bright yellow head and underparts that contrasts with a gray or black back, though in winter plumage and for females, the yellow is paler and the head can be duller with a dark cheek patch. It is named for its citrine (yellow) plumage and its characteristic constantly wagging tail. This insectivorous bird is found near water, such as marshes and lake edges, where it forages for insects by picking them off vegetation or wading into shallow water.
column12: Breeding Plumage: The most recognizable male in breeding plumage has a brilliant yellow head and underparts. Upperparts: Gray or black upperparts, depending on the subspecies, with a distinct black band on the nape. Winter Plumage: Less conspicuous than in breeding plumage, but still more vibrant than the female, with bright yellow underparts that can be diluted by white.
column13: Overall Appearance: A more subdued, "washed-out" version of the male in winter plumage. Head: A duller head with a yellowish supercilium (a stripe above the eye) and a dark cheek patch. Underparts: Paler yellow or whitish underparts and undertail coverts.
column15: The Citrine Wagtail is found as a migratory winter visitor and partial resident in India, with its range extending across the entire subcontinent, including Kashmir, the North East (Assam, Arunachal Pradesh), parts of the Himalayas, and down into the Indian peninsula.
column17:
Motacilla citreola citreola
Motacilla citreola werae
Motacilla citreola calcarata
column18: Eastern Yellow Wagtail

column11: The Common Hawk-Cuckoo is a medium-sized, ash-grey bird, easily mistaken for a hawk due to its hawk-like appearance, yellow eye rim, and mimicry of hawk flight and calls. Its plumage features a grey- brown back and a whitish, cross-barred underbelly and tail. Known as the "brainfever bird," its loud, repetitive "brain-fever" call, which sounds like "brain-fever," is famously produced by the male during the breeding season. This brood parasite lays its eggs in the nests of other birds, like babblers, and feeds on insects, including hairy caterpillars
column12: Produce the loud, distinctive, three- note "brainfever" call during the breeding season. Generally have ashy-gray upperparts.
column13: Make a different sound, described as a series of grating notes or a bubbling sound. Females & Juveniles are often browner than males, with bolder streaking on the underparts.
column15: across most of the Indian subcontinent, including states in North India, Central India, and Peninsular India
column17:
Hierococcyx varius varius
Hierococcyx varius ciceliae
column18: Plaintive Cuckoo

column11: The Common Kingfisher is a small, brightly colored bird known for its striking blue and orange plumage, long pointed bill, and compact body with short legs. It inhabits rivers, streams, and other wetlands with small fish, often perched on branches over the water. Easily identified by its shrill call, low, fast flight as a turquoise flash, and impressive diving and fishing skills, it is a shy bird despite its vibrant colors.
column12: The male has a completely black bill, which is a key distinguishing feature. He is slightly larger than the female, though the overall appearance of both sexes is otherwise quite similar.
column13: The female is identified by her bill, which has an orange-red lower mandible with a black tip, or in some cases, orange over most of the lower mandible. She is slightly smaller than the male but closely resembles him in general appearance.
column15: The Common Kingfisher is widely distributed across entire India.
column17:

Alcedo atthis ispida
Alcedo atthis bengalensis

column11: The Common Sandpiper is a small shorebird with greyish-brown upperparts, white underparts, and short yellowish legs. Key identification features include its stiff-winged flight over water, distinctive wingbar in flight, and a bill with a pale base and dark tip. It is fully migratory, breeding in Europe and Asia, and wintering in Africa, India, and other parts of the southern Pacific. This solitary bird forages by sight for invertebrates on the ground or in shallow water.
column12: Both males and females have similar brown upperparts and white underparts
column13: Females are generally larger than males, a difference that can be quite significant during the egg-laying season when their weight increases substantially.
column15: The Common Sandpiper is a migratory bird that winters throughout the Indian subcontinent, including states across the country, preferring wetlands, riverbanks, and estuaries.
column17: Monotype

column11: The Cotton Pygmy-Goose is a tiny, duck-like waterfowl found in tropical Asia and Australia, named for the male's "cottony" white plumage. Males have a striking white head, neck, and underparts, a dark green-black cap and collar, and a dark brown back with a glossy sheen. Females are more subdued, with a duller cap, a prominent dark eye-line, and less contrast in their grayish-brown plumage.
column12: The male has a bright white head and neck with a dark, glossy blackish-green crown. Its face and underparts are white, contrasted by a prominent, dark, blackish-green collar at the base of the neck. The eyes are a vivid crimson-red, and in flight the wings display a broad, gleaming-white band that stands out against the dark wing-coverts. In non-breeding (eclipse) plumage, the male resembles the female in overall appearance but retains the broad white wing-bar as a key distinguishing feature.
column13: The female has a plainer head with a less distinct dark cap or crown patch. An obvious dark eye-stripe is present, but it does not extend to the nape. Her face is flecked, and the neck is finely vermiculated with wavy markings, in contrast to the male’s solid white areas. In flight, the wings show a less striking pattern, with only a white trailing edge to the secondaries. Unlike the male’s crimson eyes, the female has brown eyes.
column17:
Nettapus coromandelianus coromandelianus
Nettapus coromandelianus albipennis

column11: The Cotton Pygmy Goose is the smallest member of the duck family, with a compact body, short bill, and striking plumage. Males have glossy green upperparts, white face and underparts, and chestnut flanks. Females are duller, with brownish plumage and white underparts. They are often seen in pairs or small groups on lakes, ponds, and wetlands, feeding on aquatic plants and seeds.
column12: Breeding males have glossy green back, white face, chestnut flanks, and bright plumage.
column13: Females are duller, with brownish upperparts, white face stripe, and less vivid plumage.
column14: Soft whistles and quacks; generally quiet.
column15: Widely distributed across South and Southeast Asia, including India, Bangladesh, Nepal, Sri Lanka, Myanmar, and Thailand. In West Bengal, found in village ponds, wetlands, and lakes.
column17: Monotypic (no recognized subspecies).
column18: Comb Duck (Sarkidiornis melanotos) – larger; Lesser Whistling Duck (Dendrocygna javanica).
column19: Smallest duck in the world; highly aquatic, rarely seen walking on land; nests in tree cavities near water.
column20: Images show tiny duck with green back and white face floating on ponds.

column11: The Common Teal is a small dabbling duck, one of the most widespread migratory waterfowl. It has a compact body, short neck, and swift flight. It feeds on aquatic plants, seeds, and small invertebrates, often seen dabbling in shallow waters. In winter, large flocks gather in wetlands, lakes, and paddy fields.
column12: Breeding males have chestnut head with broad green eye patch, grey body, and yellowish rear.
column13: Females are mottled brown with pale underparts, resembling other female dabbling ducks.
column14: Soft whistles and short quacks; males give clear “peep” notes.
column15: Breeds across northern Eurasia. Winters in South Asia, including India, Bangladesh, Nepal, and Sri Lanka. In West Bengal, common in wetlands, rivers, and paddy fields during winter migration.
column17: Several subspecies, including A. c. crecca (Eurasia), A. c. carolinensis (North America, Green-winged Teal).
column18: Garganey (Anas querquedula) – larger, with white eye stripe; Eurasian Wigeon (Mareca penelope).
column19: Highly gregarious in winter; important indicator of wetland health; hunted in some regions.
column20: Images show small duck with green eye patch (male) or mottled brown plumage (female) in wetlands.

column11: The Common Kestrel is a small falcon with rufous-brown upperparts, black spots, and a long tail with a black terminal band. It is known for its hovering flight while hunting small mammals, birds, and insects. It prefers open countryside, grasslands, and agricultural fields.
column12: Males have grey head and tail, rufous back with black spots, and black terminal tail band.
column13: Females are larger, with brown head and tail, more heavily streaked plumage.
column14: High-pitched “kee-kee-kee” calls, especially during breeding season.
column15: Widely distributed across Europe, Asia, and Africa. In India, including West Bengal, it is a winter visitor found in open fields, grasslands, and rural landscapes.
column17: Several subspecies across its wide range (e.g., F. t. tinnunculus in Europe, F. t. rupicolus in South Asia).
column18: Lesser Kestrel (Falco naumanni) – smaller, lacks heavy spotting; Eurasian Hobby (Falco subbuteo).
column19: Famous for hovering flight while hunting; plays ecological role in controlling rodent populations.
column20: Images show small falcon hovering in fields or perched with rufous back and grey head (male).

column11: The Eastern Yellow Wagtail is a small, slender bird, known for its characteristic long, constantly wagging tail and olive-green back and yellow underparts in breeding males. Key features include a black tail with white outer feathers, a long hind claw, and a high-pitched call. Breeding males exhibit striking head patterns and a gray crown, while non-breeding birds and females are duller, though the tail and yellow may be diluted with white. They are ground-foraging insectivores.
column12: During breeding males have bright yellow underparts and throat. Greenish or olive-green upperparts. Head color varies by subspecies, ranging from gray to white. A distinct white line above the eye (supercilium). Outer tail feathers are white.
column13: During breeding females are duller, with less yellow on the underparts and more white. Head is often paler and may have a brownish tinge. Can have a more pronounced gray necklace.
column15: The Eastern Yellow Wagtail is found throughout India during its non-breeding (winter) season, from the northern states like Jammu and Kashmir and Assam to the southern tip of the peninsula, with specific sightings confirmed in states such as Kerala, Karnataka, West Bengal, and Tripura. It also occurs in the Andaman and Narcondam Islands.

column11: The Eastern Cattle Egret is a small, stocky white heron often seen in association with grazing livestock. It feeds on insects and small animals disturbed by cattle and tractors. In breeding plumage, it develops golden-buff plumes on the head, breast, and back. It is highly adaptable, thriving in wetlands, grasslands, agricultural fields, and even urban areas.
column12: Breeding males show golden-buff plumes on head, breast, and back, with reddish bill and legs.
column13: Females resemble males; juveniles are plain white with yellow bill and legs.
column14: Harsh “rick-rick” and croaking calls, especially in colonies.
column15: Widely distributed across South and Southeast Asia, including India, Bangladesh, Nepal, Sri Lanka, Myanmar, Thailand, Cambodia, Vietnam, and China. Often found in agricultural fields, wetlands, and near cattle.
column17: B. c. coromandus
column18: Western Cattle Egret (Bubulcus ibis), Little Egret (Egretta garzetta)

column11: The Eurasian Collared-Dove is a medium-sized dove with pale grey-buff plumage, a slender body, and a long square-tipped tail. Its most distinctive feature is the narrow black collar on the nape, bordered with white. It is highly adaptable, thriving in urban, rural, and agricultural landscapes. It feeds on grains, seeds, and small fruits, often foraging near human habitation.
column12: Males and females are alike, both showing pale grey plumage and black collar.
column13: Females resemble males; juveniles lack the distinct collar and are duller overall.
column14: Soft, repetitive three-note cooing “coo-COO-coo,” often heard in pairs or flocks.
column15: Native to Europe and Asia; widely distributed across South Asia including India, Bangladesh, Nepal, and Sri Lanka. Introduced populations thrive in North America and other regions.
column17: S. d. decaocto, S. d. xanthocyclus
column18: Red Collared-Dove (Streptopelia tranquebarica), Laughing Dove (Spilopelia senegalensis)

column8: Migratory (breeds in Europe/Asia, winters in South Asia/Africa)
column11: The Eurasian Hoopoe is a distinctive bird with cinnamon-orange plumage, bold black-and-white wings, and a long, slender, down-curved bill. Its most striking feature is the erectile crest tipped with black, which fans out when excited. It feeds mainly on insects, grubs, and small invertebrates, probing the ground with its bill. Often seen in open fields, grasslands, and rural areas.
column12: Males are cinnamon-orange with black-and-white wings and a prominent crest.
column13: Females resemble males but may appear slightly duller; juveniles have shorter crests and paler plumage.
column14: Soft “hoo-poo-poo” calls, repeated in series; alarm calls are harsher.
column15: Widely distributed across Europe, Asia, and North Africa. In South Asia, including India and Bangladesh, it is a winter visitor and passage migrant.
column17: U. e. epops, U. e. africana, U. e. asiatica, U. e. senegalensis
column18: Indian Hoopoe (Upupa indica – sometimes treated as subspecies), Woodpeckers (superficially similar in crest and bill use)

column11: The Eurasian Moorhen is a medium-sized waterbird with dark brownish-black plumage, white undertail coverts, and a distinctive red bill with yellow tip. It has long greenish legs with unwebbed toes, adapted for walking on floating vegetation. It is omnivorous, feeding on aquatic plants, insects, and small fish. Often seen swimming in ponds, marshes, and wetlands, flicking its white undertail feathers.
column12: Males are similar to females, both showing dark plumage and red bill.
column13: Females resemble males; juveniles are dull brown with paler underparts and lack the bright bill coloration.
column14: Loud, harsh “kurr-kurr” and “krek-krek” calls; also emits sharp alarm notes.
column15: Widely distributed across Europe, Asia, and Africa. In South Asia, including India, Bangladesh, Nepal, and Sri Lanka, it is common in wetlands, ponds, and rice fields.
column17: G. c. chloropus, G. c. orientalis, G. c. pyrrhorrhoa
column18: Common Coot (Fulica atra), Purple Swamphen (Porphyrio poliocephalus)

column8: Migratory (breeds in northern Eurasia, winters in South Asia)
column11: The Eurasian Wigeon is a medium-sized dabbling duck with a rounded head and short bill. It is often seen in large flocks on lakes, marshes, and coastal wetlands during winter. It feeds mainly on aquatic plants and grasses, grazing in shallow water or on land.
column12: Breeding males have a chestnut head with creamy-yellow forehead, grey body, pinkish breast, and white wing patch visible in flight.
column13: Females are mottled brown with a paler face and less distinct markings; juveniles resemble females.
column14: Whistling “wee-oo” notes by males; females give low growls and quacks.
column15: Breeds across northern Europe and Asia. Winters in South Asia, including India, Bangladesh, Nepal, and Sri Lanka, as well as Africa and the Middle East. Prefers lakes, reservoirs, marshes, and coastal lagoons.
column17: Monotypic (no widely recognized subspecies).
column18: American Wigeon (Mareca americana), Gadwall (Mareca strepera)

column8: Migratory (breeds in Europe/Asia, winters in Africa/South Asia)
column11: The Eurasian Wryneck is a small, cryptically patterned woodpecker with mottled brown, grey, and buff plumage that provides excellent camouflage against tree bark. Unlike other woodpeckers, it does not drum but has a long, flexible tongue for feeding on ants and insects. Its name comes from its ability to twist its head in a snake-like manner when threatened.
column12: Males are mottled brown and grey with subtle barring and streaks; plumage is cryptic and similar to females.
column13: Females resemble males; juveniles are duller with heavier streaking.
column14: Loud, repeated “quee-quee-quee” calls; alarm calls are harsh and rasping.
column15: Breeds across Europe and northern Asia; winters in Africa, the Middle East, and South Asia including India. Found in open woodlands, orchards, and scrublands.
column17: J. t. torquilla, J. t. chinensis, J. t. sarudnyi, J. t. tschusii
column18: Grey-headed Woodpecker (Picus canus), Lesser Spotted Woodpecker (Dryobates minor)

column11: A medium-sized woodpecker with buffy underparts, black-and-white barred wings, and a distinctive fulvous (yellowish-buff) breast. It has a strong bill for drilling into wood and feeds on insects, larvae, and occasionally fruits. Often seen clinging to tree trunks and branches in forests, groves, and wooded gardens.
column12: Males have a red crown patch along with buff breast and black-and-white barred wings.
column13: Females lack the red crown patch but otherwise resemble males; juveniles are duller with less distinct barring.
column14: Sharp “kik-kik” calls and drumming on tree trunks.
column15: Found across the Indian subcontinent, including India, Bangladesh, Nepal, Bhutan, and Myanmar. Prefers forests, wooded areas, plantations, and urban groves.
column17: D. m. macei, D. m. himalayensis
column18: Black-rumped Flameback (Dinopium benghalense), Lesser Yellownape (Picus chlorolophus)

column11: The Glossy Ibis is a medium-sized wading bird with long legs and a slender, down-curved bill. Its plumage appears dark at a distance but shines with bronze, green, and purple iridescence in good light. It feeds in shallow wetlands, probing mud for insects, crustaceans, and small fish.
column12: Males and females look alike; both have iridescent plumage and long decurved bill.
column13: Females are similar to males; sexual dimorphism is minimal.
column14: Nasal, grunting “grrr” calls, often in colonies.
column15: Widely distributed across Europe, Africa, Asia, and Australia. In India, including West Bengal, it is a winter visitor found in wetlands, marshes, and paddy fields.
column17: Monotypic (no recognized subspecies).
column18: Black-headed Ibis (Threskiornis melanocephalus) – larger, white plumage; Eurasian Curlew (Numenius arquata) – longer bill, different plumage.
column19: Often breeds in colonies with other herons and ibises; highly gregarious in winter.
column20: Images show dark ibis with bronze-green iridescence, long legs, and decurved bill in wetlands.

column11: A large, striking rail with purplish-blue body, gray head, and long red bill with frontal shield. It has long reddish legs and unwebbed toes, adapted for walking on floating vegetation. It feeds on aquatic plants, shoots, and small animals. Often seen in wetlands, ponds, and marshes, flicking its tail to reveal white undertail coverts.
column12: Males are similar to females, both showing gray head and purplish-blue body.
column13: Females resemble males; juveniles are duller with brownish tones and less vivid coloration.
column14: Loud, harsh “korr-korr” and “kek-kek” calls; also emits sharp alarm notes.
column15: Found across South Asia, including India, Bangladesh, Nepal, and Sri Lanka. Prefers wetlands, marshes, ponds, and rice fields.
column17: Monotypic (no widely recognized subspecies).
column18: Purple Swamphen (Porphyrio porphyrio), Eurasian Moorhen (Gallinula chloropus)

column11: A large, crow-like cuckoo with glossy black head, neck, and body, contrasted by rich chestnut wings. It has a long, broad tail and red eyes. Prefers dense vegetation, scrub, and gardens, where it skulks low to the ground. Feeds on insects, caterpillars, small reptiles, and occasionally fruits. Known for its deep, resonant calls.
column12: Males are glossy black with chestnut wings and long black tail.
column13: Females resemble males; juveniles are streaked brown and buff, with duller plumage.
column14: Deep, resonant “coop-coop-coop” calls, often repeated in series.
column15: Widely distributed across South and Southeast Asia, including India, Bangladesh, Nepal, Sri Lanka, Myanmar, Thailand, Cambodia, and southern China. Found in scrublands, forests, and urban gardens.
column17: C. s. sinensis, C. s. bubutus, C. s. intermedius
column18: Lesser Coucal (Centropus bengalensis), Crow Pheasant (local name for Greater Coucal itself)

column8: Migratory (breeds in temperate Eurasia, winters in South Asia)
column11: A small, active leaf warbler with greenish upperparts, whitish underparts, and a faint wing bar. It is restless, flitting among foliage in forests and gardens, feeding on insects and spiders. Known for its complex song and subtle plumage differences across populations.
column12: Males are similar to females, both showing greenish upperparts and whitish underparts; males sing prominently during breeding season.
column13: Females resemble males; juveniles are duller with less distinct wing markings.
column14: High-pitched, varied trills and warbling notes; song is complex and melodious.
column15: Breeds across northern and central Eurasia; winters in South Asia including India, Bangladesh, Nepal, and Sri Lanka. Found in forests, woodlands, and gardens.
column17: P. t. trochiloides, P. t. viridanus, P. t. ludlowi, P. t. plumbeitarsus
column18: Hume’s Leaf Warbler (Phylloscopus humei), Arctic Warbler (Phylloscopus borealis)

column11: A slender wagtail with long tail, grey upperparts, yellow underparts, and white supercilium. It is often seen near fast-flowing rivers, streams, and wetlands, constantly wagging its tail. Feeds mainly on insects and aquatic invertebrates.

column11: The gadwall is a medium-sized, drab brown duck found worldwide in freshwater wetlands. A white wing patch is also present, though it is smaller and less prominent than in the male.

column11: The Asian Paradise-Flycatcher is a medium-sized, insectivorous bird known for the adult male's long, ribbon-like tail streamers and two color morphs—a snowy white morph and a cinnamon-colored morph, both with a glossy black crested head. They are found in wooded country and shady groves, hunting insects by sallying from prominent perches in the air.

Its head is glossy black and crested, often enhanced by a delicate blue ring around the eye.
column13: The female Asian Paradise-flycatcher has a much shorter tail, lacking the long, flowing streamers of the male. She is generally rufous on the upperparts, with a darker and less glossy head compared to the male’s crest. Her throat is marked by a distinctive greyish patch, making her more subdued in appearance.
column15: The Indian Paradise Flycatcher is found widespread throughout India, from the Himalayan foothills to the southern peninsula. It is a migratory species, with populations from northern India moving south for winter, while some southern birds are year-round residents

column11: The Pheasant-tailed Jacana is a unique, long-tailed wader of tropical Asian wetlands, distinguished by its ability to walk on water-lily pads using oversized feet. In breeding plumage, the male boasts a long, striking, pheasant-like tail, while females are more colorful and exhibit polyandry, maintaining harems of males. They inhabit marshes and lakes with floating vegetation, feeding on insects and small aquatic creatures.

column11: The Taiga Flycatcher is a small, insectivorous bird breeding in the boreal forests of Eurasia and wintering in Southeast Asia. The breeding male has a distinct orange-red throat patch, surrounded by gray. The non-breeding male has less orange on the throat and darker flanks. Females are cold brown above with a dirty-white belly and lack the male's red throat. The bird has a dark bill and a characteristic black-and-white tail

Its head and upperparts are a browner color, while the uppertail-coverts are distinctly blacker, giving it a sharper contrast. The non-breeding male shows a greatly reduced or entirely absent red-orange throat patch. Its flanks are more buffish and darker than those of the breeding male, making it appear duller overall.

column11: The Tufted Duck is a medium-sized, black-and-white diving duck named for the distinctive tassel or crest of feathers on the back of the head, especially prominent in males. column11: White Wagtail has a slender body with a distinctive long, constantly wagging tail and bold black, white, and gray plumage that varies across its vast Eurasian and North African range. It's easily identified by its characteristic tail- pumping movement and is often found alone, in pairs, or small groups.
column12: Breeding Plumage: A prominent black crown and nape, a distinctive black throat bib, and black markings on the belly. The black is deep and contrasts sharply with white and grey areas. Non-breeding Plumage: The black markings, particularly on the head and throat, become duller and more greyish, and some white appears on the forehead.
column13: Breeding Plumage: The head is duller and more greyish than the male's. The black areas are replaced by dark or medium grey. Non-breeding Plumage: The crown and nape are washed with olive-grey, with the chin, throat, and breast appearing white with some black mottling.
column15: The White Wagtail is found throughout India in open wetlands and urban environments, extending from Gujarat and Ladakh to the southern tip of the peninsula and the Northeast states like Arunachal Pradesh, though it is scarce in the south.
column17:
Motacilla alba alba
Motacilla alba dukhunensis
Motacilla alba yarrellii
Motacilla alba ocularis
Motacilla alba subpersonata
Motacilla alba personata
Motacilla alba baicalensis
Motacilla alba lugens
Motacilla alba leucopsis
Motacilla alba alboides

column11: The White-throated Kingfisher is a medium-sized, striking bird with a chestnut-brown body, a bright electric-blue back and wings, and a prominent snow-white patch from its throat across its breast. It has a large head, a long, heavy, pointed bill, and red legs. Juvenile birds are a duller version of the adult, with dark scaly markings on the neck and breast.
column12: Females tend to have a slightly greener back and wings.
column13: Males have bluer back and wings.
column15: White-throated Kingfisher is widely distributed across almost all Indian states, from the Himalayan foothills to the peninsula, and can be found from Uttarakhand to West Bengal and down through the western part of the peninsula.
column17:
Halcyon smyrnensis smyrnensis
Halcyon smyrnensis fusca
Halcyon smyrnensis saturatior
Halcyon smyrnensis perpulchra
Halcyon smyrnensis fokiensis
column19: The White-throated Kingfisher is the state bird of West Bengal.

column11: The Western Yellow Wagtail is a small, slender bird with bright yellow underparts and olive-green upperparts. It is highly migratory, breeding in Europe and Central Asia and wintering in South Asia, including India. It is often seen in open fields, wetlands, and agricultural landscapes, walking or running while wagging its tail.
column12: Breeding males have bright yellow underparts, olive-green back, and variable head patterns depending on subspecies.
column13: Females and non-breeding males are duller, with paler yellow underparts and less distinct head markings.
column14: High-pitched “tswee-tswee” calls; sharp flight notes.
column15: Breeds across Europe and Central Asia; winters in South Asia, including India, Bangladesh, Nepal, and Sri Lanka. In West Bengal, common in paddy fields, wetlands, and open countryside during winter migration.
column17: Numerous subspecies (e.g., M. f. flava, M. f. thunbergi, M. f. beema) with varying head patterns.
column18: Eastern Yellow Wagtail (Motacilla tschutschensis), Citrine Wagtail (Motacilla citreola).
column19: Highly variable plumage; important indicator of seasonal migration; feeds on insects in open habitats.
column20: Images show bright yellow wagtail in fields or wetlands, often wagging tail.

column11: The White-breasted Waterhen is a medium-sized, rail-like bird with slate-grey body, white face, throat, and breast, and reddish undertail coverts. It has long yellowish legs and a short tail often flicked while walking. It is commonly found in marshes, wetlands, paddy fields, and village ponds, feeding on insects, mollusks, seeds, and small aquatic creatures.
column12: Males and females look alike; both have slate-grey plumage, white breast, and reddish undertail.
column13: Females are similar to males; sexual dimorphism is minimal.
column14: Loud, harsh “kroak-kroak” calls, especially at dawn and dusk.
column15: Widely distributed across South and Southeast Asia, including India, Bangladesh, Nepal, Sri Lanka, Myanmar, Thailand, and Indonesia. Very common in wetlands and paddy fields of West Bengal.
column17: Monotypic (no recognized subspecies).
column18: Brown Crake (Zapornia akool) – darker overall; Ruddy-breasted Crake (Zapornia fusca) – smaller, rufous breast.
column19: Bold and conspicuous compared to other crakes; often seen walking openly near water edges.
column20: Images show slate-grey waterbird with white breast and reddish undertail near wetlands.

column11: The Yellow Bittern is a small, secretive heron with buff-yellow plumage, chestnut crown, and pale underparts. It has a short neck and is often seen skulking in reed beds and marshes. It feeds on fish, frogs, insects, and aquatic invertebrates. Its cryptic coloration makes it difficult to spot, as it often freezes with neck stretched upward, resembling reeds.
column12: Males have richer chestnut crown and darker flight feathers; overall buff-yellow plumage.
column13: Females are paler, with less chestnut on crown and duller wings.
column14: Soft “krek-krek” calls, often at dusk.
column15: Breeds across East and South Asia, including China, Japan, and Southeast Asia. Migrates southwards in winter. In India, including West Bengal, it is a winter visitor found in marshes, wetlands, and paddy fields.
column17: Monotypic (no recognized subspecies).
column18: Little Bittern (Ixobrychus minutus) – smaller, darker; Cinnamon Bittern (Ixobrychus cinnamomeus) – more rufous overall.
column19: Very secretive; often detected only when flushed from reeds. Breeds in dense marsh vegetation.
column20: Images show small buff-yellow heron with chestnut crown, standing among reeds.

column11: The Yellow-bellied Prinia is a small, active warbler with olive-brown upperparts, bright yellow underparts, and a long tail often held cocked. It is commonly found in grasslands, scrub, and reed beds near wetlands. It feeds on insects and small invertebrates, moving restlessly among vegetation.
column12: Breeding males have brighter yellow underparts and may sing from exposed perches.
column13: Females are similar to males, slightly duller in plumage.
column14: Sharp “tzee-tzee” notes, often repeated rapidly.
column15: Found across South and Southeast Asia, including India, Bangladesh, Nepal, Myanmar, Thailand, Cambodia, and Vietnam. In West Bengal, common in reed beds and grasslands near wetlands.
column17: Several subspecies, including P. f. flaviventris (India), P. f. sonitans (Myanmar).
column18: Plain Prinia (Prinia inornata) – lacks bright yellow belly; Ashy Prinia (Prinia socialis).
column19: Builds nests in grass or reeds; highly vocal and conspicuous despite small size.
column20: Images show small warbler with olive-brown back and bright yellow belly in grasslands.

column11: The Yellow-footed Green Pigeon is a medium-sized, beautifully colored pigeon with greenish-yellow plumage, lilac shoulder patch, and bright yellow legs. It is the state bird of Maharashtra. It feeds mainly on fruits, berries, and figs, often seen in flocks perched on fruiting trees. Its flight is swift and direct, and it plays an important role in seed dispersal.
column12: Males have brighter plumage with lilac shoulder patch and yellow legs.
column13: Females are similar but slightly duller, with less pronounced shoulder patch.
column14: Soft, mellow “coo-coo” calls.
column15: Widely distributed across the Indian subcontinent, including India, Bangladesh, Nepal, and Sri Lanka. In West Bengal, found in forests, groves, and fruiting trees in rural areas.
column17: Several subspecies, including T. p. phoenicoptera (India), T. p. chlorigaster (Sri Lanka).
column18: Green Imperial Pigeon (Ducula aenea) – larger, darker; Thick-billed Green Pigeon (Treron curvirostra).
column19: Important seed disperser; highly social, often seen in flocks; prefers fruiting fig trees.
column20: Images show green pigeon with lilac shoulder patch and yellow legs perched on fruiting tree.

column11: The Zitting Cisticola is a tiny, active warbler with streaked brown upperparts, whitish underparts, and a short, rounded tail. It is often seen in grasslands, scrub, and paddy fields, flitting restlessly and giving its characteristic “zitting” call during display flights. It feeds mainly on insects and small invertebrates.
column12: Breeding males perform aerial display flights while giving repetitive “zitting” calls; plumage similar to females but often fresher.
column13: Females are similar to males; sexual dimorphism is minimal.
column14: Distinctive repetitive “zitting” call during flight display.
column15: Widely distributed across Asia, Africa, and Europe. In India, including West Bengal, common in grasslands, scrub, and agricultural fields.
column17: Numerous subspecies across its wide range (e.g., C. j. cursitans in India).
column18: Plain Prinia (Prinia inornata) – larger, lacks streaked back.
column19: Very common and conspicuous during breeding season; nests built low in grass.
column20: Images show small streaked brown warbler perched in grasslands or flying with tail short and rounded.
